writing_task: Hey, I need to send an email to a key customer — Priya at Northfield Logistics — letting her know that a feature she requested six months ago is finally shipping next week. The tricky part is she's been waiting a long time and I want to acknowledge that without being overly apologetic or making it weird. She'll need to coordinate with her IT team to enable it, so there's a clear action item on her end. Can you draft something short — two or three paragraphs?

drafts.rubric:
Subject: [Feature Name] shipping next week — action needed from your IT team

Hi Priya,

The feature you requested back in March is shipping next week, and I wanted to make sure you had enough lead time to get ahead of it. You've been waiting on this for a while, and I know you've had to work around its absence — that changes on [specific date].

To turn it on, your IT team will need to enable it in your account settings before [deadline date]. I'm happy to send them the setup documentation directly if that's easier — just say the word.

I'll follow up on [date] to confirm everything is live. Reach me at [email/phone] if anything comes up before then.
drafts.generic:
Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Almost Here

Hi Priya,

Good news — the feature you requested back in the spring is officially shipping next week. We know it's been a longer road than any of us anticipated, and we genuinely appreciate your patience while we got it right.

To get things up and running on your end, you'll want to loop in your IT team ahead of the release. They'll need to enable the feature through your admin settings — I can send over step-by-step instructions whenever you're ready.

Let me know if you have questions. We're glad this one is finally landing.
drafts.preference:
Subject: Your Requested Feature Ships Next Week

Hi Priya,

Good news — the feature you requested is finally rolling out next week. It's been a longer wait than either of us would have liked, and I appreciate your patience while we got it across the finish line.

To activate it, your IT team will need to enable it on your end. I'd recommend looping them in now so you're ready to go on launch day.

Let me know if you have questions or want to walk through the setup together. Happy to jump on a quick call.

user_reason: C is the closest to what I'd actually send. It acknowledges the wait without dwelling on it, frames the impact from Priya's perspective ('you've had to work around its absence'), and gives her a clear owner and deadline rather than a vague 'loop in your IT team.' The closing is forward-looking and specific — it doesn't trail off. A is decent and clean, but 'I appreciate your patience' centers me, not her, and 'Happy to jump on a quick call' as a closer is weak — it's sentiment after the CTA. B is the worst. 'We genuinely appreciate your patience while we got it right' is tone-deaf — that's entirely about us. 'We're glad this one is finally landing' as a closing is exactly the kind of trailing sentiment that kills the momentum. Also 'we' throughout feels like it's diffusing accountability.

criteria_analysis.name: Audience-Facing Reasoning
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names what Priya has been doing ('had to work around its absence') and ties the reasoning to her experience with specificity. The generic draft gestures vaguely at patience and a 'longer road' without anchoring in Priya's situation. The original preference draft is slightly better than generic ('longer wait than either of us would have liked') but still lacks specificity about Priya's experience.

criteria_analysis.name: Operational Endings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft closes with a specific follow-up date and a clear contact channel — the reader knows exactly what happens next. The generic draft ends with 'We're glad this one is finally landing,' which is motivational filler that deflates momentum. The original preference draft offers a call but lacks a committed follow-up date, making it softer but still operational.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete Accountability
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names the IT team with a deliverable (enable in account settings) and a deadline, offers to send documentation directly, and commits to a follow-up date with contact info. Both the generic and original preference drafts mention the IT team but attach no deadline and provide no clear contact routing or follow-up commitment.

criteria_analysis.name: Direct, Non-Alarmist Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft states facts plainly without hedging or over-apologizing — 'that changes on [specific date]' is confident and clean. The generic draft hedges with 'a longer road than any of us anticipated' and 'we genuinely appreciate your patience,' which reads slightly defensive. The original preference draft uses 'finally rolling out' and 'across the finish line,' which are mildly hedging/apologetic but not egregious.

criteria_analysis.name: Signal-to-Noise Discipline
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is tightly scoped — every sentence is either information Priya needs or an action she can take. The generic draft includes 'we genuinely appreciate your patience' and 'We're glad this one is finally landing,' which serve the sender's comfort more than the reader's needs. The original preference draft is lean but 'across the finish line' is vestigial phrasing that doesn't add value.

criteria_analysis.name: Lean Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: All three drafts use prose appropriately for the content complexity. The rubric-guided and original preference drafts are clean prose with no manufactured structure. The generic draft is also prose but slightly more padded, which keeps it from a 5.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names what Priya has been doing ('had to work around its absence') and ties the reasoning to her experience with specificity. The generic draft gestures vaguely at patience and a 'longer road' without anchoring in Priya's situation. The original preference draft is slightly better than generic ('longer wait than either of us would have liked') but still lacks specificity about Priya's experience.

The generic draft ends with 'We're glad this one is finally landing,' which is motivational filler that deflates momentum. The original preference draft offers a call but lacks a committed follow-up date, making it softer but still operational.

Both the generic and original preference drafts mention the IT team but attach no deadline and provide no clear contact routing or follow-up commitment.

The rubric-guided draft states facts plainly without hedging or over-apologizing — 'that changes on [specific date]' is confident and clean. The generic draft hedges with 'a longer road than any of us anticipated' and 'we genuinely appreciate your patience,' which reads slightly defensive. The original preference draft uses 'finally rolling out' and 'across the finish line,' which are mildly hedging/apologetic but not egregious.

The generic draft includes 'we genuinely appreciate your patience' and 'We're glad this one is finally landing,' which serve the sender's comfort more than the reader's needs. The original preference draft is lean but 'across the finish line' is vestigial phrasing that doesn't add value.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion — it names Priya's specific experience, provides concrete accountability with deadlines and follow-up commitments, and ends operationally without motivational filler. The original preference draft is competent and lean but lacks the specificity and accountability structures this user consistently demands. The generic draft falls short on nearly every priority criterion, relying on sender-facing gratitude and motivational closings that this user has explicitly rejected in past conversations.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear with specific placeholders for dates, a concrete action item, and a defined follow-up plan. Draft B is clear but slightly vaguer — 'whenever you're ready' and 'admin settings' are less precise than Draft A's specifics.
Both drafts flow logically from announcement to action needed to next steps. Each paragraph builds naturally on the previous one in both versions.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors and typos, with clean, professional prose throughout.

Draft A has a tighter structure: announcement, specific action with deadline, offer to help, follow-up commitment with contact info. Draft B is well-organized but lacks a concrete deadline and follow-up plan, making it slightly less actionable.
Draft A engages by acknowledging the recipient's specific pain point ('had to work around its absence') and offering proactive next steps. Draft B is warm but slightly more generic in its engagement.
Both strike an excellent professional-yet-warm tone. Draft A is slightly more direct and action-oriented; Draft B is slightly warmer and more apologetic. Both are appropriate for the context.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A edges ahead by being more actionable and specific — it includes a deadline, a concrete follow-up date, contact information, and directly acknowledges the recipient's workaround pain. Draft B is polished and warm but is vaguer on next steps and timelines, making it less effective as a business communication that requires action.

writing_task: Hey, I need to draft a quick email to a vendor — we use their API for document parsing and their support team just told me response times are going to degrade by roughly 40% starting next month due to infrastructure changes on their end. I need to let our internal customer success team know so they can set expectations with clients before anyone gets blindsided. Keep it short — two or three paragraphs max.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Heads up — API response time degradation starting next month

You've been managing client expectations around document parsing turnaround, so I want to get this to you before it becomes a surprise.

Our document parsing vendor notified us that their infrastructure changes will slow API response times by roughly 40% beginning next month. That means any client workflow that depends on near-real-time parsing — automated ingestion, time-sensitive review queues — will see a noticeable slowdown. The degradation comes from their end, and they haven't given us a rollback timeline.

Can you flag the accounts most likely to feel this and reach out to them by end of next week?
drafts.generic:
Subject: Heads Up – API Response Time Slowdown Next Month

Hi team,

Wanted to flag something before it catches anyone off guard. Our document parsing vendor just informed us that their upcoming infrastructure changes will cause API response times to degrade by approximately 40% starting next month. This is on their end and outside our control.

I'd recommend proactively reaching out to any clients who rely on document parsing features to set expectations ahead of time. Framing it as a temporary performance change while the vendor stabilizes their infrastructure should help avoid frustration. I'll share more details as we get them.

Thanks,
[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Heads Up — API Response Time Degradation Starting Next Month

Team,

Our document parsing vendor just notified us that their infrastructure changes will cause API response times to degrade by roughly 40% beginning next month. This will likely affect how quickly we process documents for clients, so we need to get ahead of it.

Please reach out to any clients who rely on document parsing and set expectations before this hits. If you're not sure which clients this affects or need talking points, let me know and I'll help you get what you need.

user_reason: Draft B is the clear winner. It opens by acknowledging what the CS team is already doing, delivers the news with specific downstream impact framed around the reader's world (client workflows, not our operational headache), and closes with one owner and a real deadline. That's exactly the structure I want. Draft A is decent — short, professional, no jargon — but 'I'd recommend' is a soft non-action, and the closing 'I'll share more details as we get them' trails off instead of driving anything. It also frames the 'why' a bit too much from our side. Draft C is the weakest. 'Please reach out to any clients' is a group ask with no owner and no deadline — classic non-action. The last sentence is also backward; I shouldn't be offering to help with talking points in the same email where I'm assigning the task. It reads like I'm not sure who's actually responsible.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the full sequence: opens with a one-sentence acknowledgment of the reader's work ('You've been managing client expectations around document parsing turnaround'), then delivers the news, then explains why it matters from the reader's perspective (client workflows affected), then closes with a single concrete next step with a deadline. The generic draft and original preference draft both open with the news itself — no acknowledgment of the reader's situation — and the generic draft buries the 'so what' in vague framing advice rather than reader-facing impact.

criteria_analysis.name: Audience-Facing Reasoning
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names the reader's experience ('managing client expectations'), specifies what client workflows will be affected ('automated ingestion, time-sensitive review queues'), and frames impact outward. The generic draft frames the 'why' as a suggestion to the team about how to spin it ('framing it as a temporary performance change') which is sender-side process narration, not reader-facing reasoning. The original preference draft gestures at client impact ('how quickly we process documents') but stays vague and doesn't name the reader's specific situation.

criteria_analysis.name: Single Owner, Scoped Accountability
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft addresses a single implied owner ('you'), gives a clear deliverable ('flag the accounts most likely to feel this and reach out'), and attaches a deadline ('by end of next week'). The generic draft uses 'I'd recommend proactively reaching out' directed at 'team' — a classic group ask with no owner and no deadline. The original preference draft says 'Please reach out to any clients' addressed to 'Team' — same diffused responsibility problem with no deadline.

criteria_analysis.name: Operational Endings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft ends cleanly on the action item with no trailing sentiment. The generic draft ends with 'Thanks, [Your Name]' — a sign-off platitude after the CTA, plus 'I'll share more details as we get them' which is internal narration rather than a concrete next step. The original preference draft ends with an offer to help ('let me know and I'll help you get what you need') which is a reasonable operational offer but slightly soft and introduces a secondary action rather than closing on the primary one.

criteria_analysis.name: Direct, Non-Alarmist Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is plain and factual — states the degradation, its source, and its implications without hedging or catastrophizing. The generic draft includes 'This is on their end and outside our control' which reads slightly defensive, and 'should help avoid frustration' which slightly patronizes. The original preference draft is mostly direct and clean, though 'so we need to get ahead of it' slightly overstates urgency without being specific about why.

criteria_analysis.name: Signal-to-Noise Discipline
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft includes only what the reader needs: what's happening, what it affects from their perspective, and what they should do. The generic draft includes 'This is on their end and outside our control' (internal defensiveness), coaching on how to frame it to clients (sender-side process), and 'I'll share more details as we get them' (announces a future action that's not actionable now). The original preference draft is leaner but 'If you're not sure which clients this affects or need talking points, let me know' adds a conditional offer that may not be needed.

criteria_analysis.name: Lean Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: All three drafts use prose appropriately for the content complexity. The rubric-guided draft is the most disciplined — three tight paragraphs, no manufactured structure. The generic and original preference drafts are also prose-based and appropriately lean, though neither is quite as tight.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the full sequence: opens with a one-sentence acknowledgment of the reader's work ('You've been managing client expectations around document parsing turnaround'), then delivers the news, then explains why it matters from the reader's perspective (client workflows affected), then closes with a single concrete next step with a deadline. rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is clearly the best — it's the only one that follows the user's non-negotiable sequence (acknowledge → news → why → action), addresses a single owner with a deadline, and ends operationally with no trailing sentiment. The original preference draft is a solid second — cleaner tone and leaner than the generic draft — but fails on sequencing and accountability. The generic draft has the most problems: group ask, defensive framing, sender-side coaching, and a platitude closing.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear, specifying the exact impact (near-real-time parsing, automated ingestion, time-sensitive review queues) and making a concrete ask. Draft B is clear but more general, leaving the reader to figure out which clients and workflows are affected.

Draft A follows a tight logical progression: context → problem → impact → specific action request. Draft B flows well but the transition from problem description to recommendation is slightly less tightly linked.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors, typos, and mechanical issues.

Draft A has a crisp three-paragraph structure (why you're getting this email, what the problem is, what I need from you). Draft B is well-organized but slightly less purposeful in its paragraph breaks.

Draft A hooks the reader immediately with a personalized opening that explains why they specifically should care, and ends with a concrete deadline-driven ask. Draft B reads more like a generic broadcast update and is less compelling.

Draft A strikes an excellent balance — direct, professional, and respectful of the recipient's expertise. Draft B is appropriately professional but feels somewhat impersonal with the 'Hi team' opening and generic sign-off, and the suggestion about 'framing it as a temporary performance change' could come across as slightly patronizing.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger email overall: it is more targeted, makes a specific and time-bound request, and respects the reader's time by being concise and purposeful. Draft B is competent but reads more like a generic team announcement and lacks the precision and directness that make Draft A especially effective.

writing_task: Hey, I need to draft a short email to a customer success manager on our team — her name is Dana — letting her know that a high-value account she owns is being flagged for churn risk by our data team. She doesn't know yet, and I want to give her a heads-up with enough context to act fast, not just drop a bomb on her.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Heads-up on Northfield account — churn risk flag

Dana,

You've been carrying this account through a tough renewal cycle, so I want to make sure you have this before it surfaces somewhere else. The data team flagged Northfield as a churn risk this week based on a significant drop in product usage over the last 60 days combined with no logged touchpoints in the past month.

If they disengage now, you lose the renewal and likely the expansion conversation you've been building toward.

Can you put together an outreach plan for Northfield by end of week and share it with me so we can align on next steps?
drafts.generic:
Subject: Heads-Up — Churn Risk Flag on [Account Name]

Hi Dana,

Wanted to give you a quick heads-up before this hits a broader channel. Our data team has flagged [Account Name] as elevated churn risk based on recent activity trends — things like [declining usage / low login frequency / support ticket volume, etc.]. They flagged it today, so this is fresh.

I know you have context on this relationship that the data doesn't capture, so I wanted you to hear it first and have a chance to assess the situation. Happy to loop in whoever would be helpful. Let me know how I can support.

Thanks, [Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Heads-Up — Churn Risk Flag on Your Account

Hi Dana,

Quick heads-up: the data team flagged one of your high-value accounts as at risk for churn. I wanted to get this to you directly before it moves further up the chain.

I don't have the full picture yet, but they've identified signals worth acting on soon. Can you take a look at the account and let me know what you're seeing on your end? If there's something we should escalate or address together, I want to move on it quickly.

Let me know what you need from me.

Thanks,
[Your Name]

user_reason: C is the only one that gets the emotional arc right — it acknowledges Dana's situation first, delivers the news, then frames the stakes from her perspective (her renewal, her expansion conversation), and ends with one clear owner and a real deadline. That's the email I'd actually send. A is direct and doesn't waste words, but it's vague and the 'let me know what you need from me' closing is trailing sentiment with no teeth. B has the most detail but it centers my reasoning ('I wanted you to hear it first') instead of Dana's stakes, the action ask is soft and ownerless, and 'Happy to loop in whoever would be helpful' is filler.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the sequence: opens by acknowledging Dana's effort on the renewal cycle (one sentence), delivers the news (churn flag), explains why it matters from her perspective (lose the renewal and expansion), then closes with one concrete ask. The generic draft opens with sender intention ('Wanted to give you a quick heads-up') — not an acknowledgment of the reader. The original preference draft also opens with sender intent ('Quick heads-up') and never provides reader-facing stakes before the ask.

criteria_analysis.name: Audience-Facing Reasoning
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names what Dana has been working toward (the renewal, the expansion conversation) and frames the stakes around her losing those outcomes — specific and outward-facing. The generic draft vaguely gestures at 'context on this relationship' but never names concrete reader stakes. The original preference draft has no 'so what' at all — it says 'signals worth acting on' without ever bridging to what Dana stands to lose, reading like a changelog the user explicitly dislikes.

criteria_analysis.name: Single Owner, Scoped Accountability
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names Dana as the owner, gives a clear deliverable (outreach plan for Northfield), and a deadline (end of week). The generic draft asks 'Let me know how I can support' — no owner, no deliverable, no deadline. The original preference draft asks Dana to 'take a look' and report back, which has an implied owner but a vague deliverable and no deadline.

criteria_analysis.name: Operational Endings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft ends with a concrete, forward-looking CTA (outreach plan by end of week) with nothing trailing after it. The generic draft ends with 'Thanks, [Your Name]' after a vague 'Let me know how I can support' — trailing sentiment and no real action. The original preference draft ends with 'Let me know what you need from me' followed by 'Thanks, [Your Name]' — the CTA is somewhat forward-looking but vague, and trailing sentiment follows it.

criteria_analysis.name: Signal-to-Noise Discipline
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft includes only information Dana needs: the flag, the data behind it, what she stands to lose, and what to do. The generic draft includes placeholder brackets and internal narration ('I know you have context the data doesn't capture') that serves the sender's positioning more than the reader's action. The original preference draft is lean but too lean — 'signals worth acting on soon' is vague filler that doesn't give Dana anything concrete to work with.

criteria_analysis.name: Lean Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: All three use prose appropriately without manufactured structure. The rubric-guided draft has short, focused paragraphs that carry cause and stakes together naturally. The generic and original preference drafts are similarly lean in structure, though the generic draft's placeholder brackets suggest unfinished formatting.

criteria_analysis.name: Direct, Non-Alarmist Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is factual and direct without catastrophizing — 'significant drop' and 'churn risk' are plain descriptions. The generic draft hedges with 'things like [declining usage / low login frequency...]' which feels evasive and vague. The original preference draft hedges with 'I don't have the full picture yet' and 'signals worth acting on' — both feel like softening rather than directness, which this user consistently flags.
